methodology: Cross-referenced FatSecret generic entries, CalorieKing, USDA FoodData Central, MyFitnessPal community averages, and Nutritionix restaurant estimates. Local Thai restaurants don't publish official nutrition facts — all values are ESTIMATES based on typical recipes and ingredient-level USDA data. Portions reflect typical restaurant servings. Coconut milk-based dishes use full-fat coconut milk (~450 cal/cup) as the baseline since that's what restaurants use. Calorie ranges account for variation in oil/coconut milk usage across restaurants.

key_insights.coconut_milk_trap: Full-fat coconut milk = ~450 cal/cup. Every curry uses 1-2 cups. This is why Thai curries are 400-600 cal before rice.
key_insights.leanest_options: Tom Yum soup (clear broth, ~120 cal/cup), Larb (minced meat salad, ~250 cal), Fresh spring rolls (~80 cal each), Papaya salad (~150 cal).
key_insights.calorie_bombs: Pad Thai (500-700 cal from sugar+oil+peanuts), Pineapple Fried Rice (600+ cal from sugar), Massaman Curry (550+ cal from coconut+potatoes+peanuts), Peanut sauce (80-100 cal per 2 tbsp).
key_insights.rice_multiplier: Every dish served WITH rice adds 200-400 cal. Ask for half rice or skip rice on curry dishes to save 200 cal instantly.
key_insights.protein_hack: Stir-fry dishes (Pad Krapow, Ginger Chicken, Garlic Pepper) are the best macro dishes — high protein, moderate cal, no coconut milk.

items.notes: Coconut milk + green curry paste + chicken + Thai basil + bamboo shoots. Restaurants use full-fat coconut milk (450 cal/cup) and extra oil. This is CURRY ONLY — add 200+ cal for jasmine rice on the side. FatSecret generic green curry ~390 cal/cup; restaurant versions are richer.
items.lean_version: Ask for light coconut milk (saves ~150 cal). Or get Tom Yum instead (clear broth, saves 300 cal). Skip rice = saves another 200 cal.

items.notes: This is how it's actually served. One cup curry + one cup steamed jasmine rice. Most people eat 1.5-2 cups rice, pushing this to 700-820 cal. The rice is the lever — half rice saves 100 cal.
items.lean_version: Half rice (saves 100 cal). Or cauliflower rice if available (saves 180 cal). Better yet: get a stir-fry instead.

items.notes: Coconut milk + red curry paste + chicken + bell peppers + bamboo shoots + Thai basil. Slightly fewer calories than green curry (less coconut milk in some recipes). Red paste has more dried red chilies. Similar macro profile to green curry.
items.lean_version: Same as green curry — ask for light coconut milk or extra vegetables instead of extra sauce. The peppers and bamboo shoots are basically free calories.

items.notes: Coconut milk + yellow curry paste (turmeric-heavy) + chicken + potatoes + onions. The potatoes add carbs compared to green/red curry. Mild and creamy — often the most coconut milk-heavy curry on the menu.
items.lean_version: Ask for extra chicken, fewer potatoes. The potatoes absorb coconut milk like sponges, adding hidden calories.

items.notes: The CALORIE KING of Thai curries. Coconut milk + Massaman paste + chicken + potatoes + peanuts + onions + cinnamon + star anise. Peanuts add fat+cal on top of already-rich coconut milk. HEB frozen version: 490 cal/tray. Restaurant versions are richer. Often served with extra peanuts on top.
items.lean_version: This is the worst curry for macros. Switch to green or red curry (saves 100-150 cal). If you must: skip the peanuts, ask for less sauce.

items.notes: Thicker and richer than standard curries — uses less liquid coconut milk but more peanut paste in the curry base. Typically served with kaffir lime leaves. No potatoes = fewer carbs than Massaman/Yellow, but the peanut paste keeps fat high.
items.lean_version: Ask for extra chicken and vegetables. Panang is already more protein-dense than Massaman since it skips potatoes.

items.notes: Rice noodles + tamarind sauce + fish sauce + SUGAR + egg + chicken + bean sprouts + peanuts + oil. FatSecret generic: 357 cal/cup but restaurant portions are 1.5-2 cups. The sugar in tamarind sauce (2-3 tbsp per serving) is the hidden bomb. Peanuts add another 80-100 cal on top.
items.lean_version: Ask for light sauce (saves 50-80 cal of sugar). Skip crushed peanuts on top (saves 80 cal). Better yet: get a stir-fry with rice noodles instead.

items.notes: Same as chicken Pad Thai but shrimp is leaner (fewer calories from protein source). Still has the sugar-heavy tamarind sauce. Shrimp Pad Thai is the 'better' Pad Thai option if you must order it.
items.lean_version: Shrimp is already the leaner protein. Skip peanuts, ask for light sauce. Best of the Pad Thai options.

items.notes: Tofu version has LESS protein than chicken or shrimp (18g vs 25-28g). Tofu is often fried before adding to Pad Thai, which adds oil calories. Not a better choice than shrimp/chicken unless you're vegetarian. Trader Joe's frozen version: 251 cal/bowl (312g) but much smaller portion.
items.lean_version: Ask for soft tofu (not fried). Still lower protein than chicken/shrimp — not the 'healthy' option people think it is.

items.notes: Wide rice noodles + dark soy sauce + oyster sauce + egg + Chinese broccoli + chicken. FatSecret: 363 cal/cup, typical plate is ~1.7 cups. The wide noodles absorb more oil than thin noodles. Soy/oyster sauce combo = high sodium (1500-2000mg). Less sugar than Pad Thai but more oil-heavy.
items.lean_version: Ask for extra Chinese broccoli, less noodle. The broccoli is basically free calories and adds fiber. Light oil request saves 50-100 cal.

items.notes: Wide rice noodles + Thai basil + chili + garlic + bell peppers + chicken + oyster sauce. Called 'drunken' noodles because spicy food is good hangover food. More vegetables than Pad See Ew but also more oil (stir-fry technique). One of the highest-calorie noodle dishes.
items.lean_version: Ask for light oil and extra vegetables. The peppers and basil add flavor without calories. Or request it with glass noodles instead of wide rice noodles (saves ~80 cal).

items.notes: Thin rice noodles in dark, rich beef or pork broth + meat + bean sprouts + morning glory. Traditional boats serve tiny bowls (50 cal each); restaurant versions are full-size. Broth is rich and aromatic but not coconut milk-based, so moderate calories. Often has blood mixed into broth (adds protein, minimal cal).
items.lean_version: Already a decent option — broth-based, not coconut. Ask for extra bean sprouts and vegetables. One of the better noodle choices.

items.notes: Glass noodles (mung bean) + egg + vegetables + chicken or shrimp + soy/oyster sauce. Glass noodles are lower calorie than rice noodles (160 cal/cup vs 190 cal/cup dry). Lighter and more translucent. Often perceived as healthier — and actually IS slightly better than Pad Thai/Pad See Ew.
items.lean_version: Already one of the lighter noodle dishes. Ask for extra vegetables. Glass noodles absorb less oil than wide rice noodles.

items.notes: Jasmine rice + egg + chicken + onion + tomato + scallions + soy sauce + oil. Simpler than Chinese fried rice — less soy, more fish sauce, served with cucumber/lime/cilantro. Calorie density depends on how much oil the wok gets. Typical Thai restaurant plate is ~2 cups.
items.lean_version: Ask for light oil. Better yet: get steamed rice + a stir-fry protein on the side (you control the portions).

items.notes: Thai fried rice + Thai basil + chili + garlic + extra soy/oyster sauce. Slightly more calorie-dense than regular Khao Pad because the basil sauce adds more oil and soy. Same base, bigger flavor, ~30 more cal.
items.lean_version: Same as regular fried rice — ask for light oil. The basil and chili add flavor without significant calories.

items.notes: CALORIE TRAP. Regular fried rice + pineapple chunks + raisins + cashews + curry powder. The pineapple adds sugar, raisins add sugar, cashews add fat. Served in a hollowed pineapple which looks Instagram-worthy but you're eating 80-100 extra calories of sugar and nuts vs regular fried rice. Often comes with shrimp.
items.lean_version: Get regular Khao Pad instead (saves 80 cal). The pineapple/raisin/cashew additions are pure sugar and fat with no protein benefit.

items.notes: The baseline. Plain steamed jasmine rice. USDA verified. Restaurants typically serve 1.5-2 cups (300-410 cal). Jasmine rice is slightly stickier and more aromatic than regular long-grain but same calories. This is the #1 calorie lever in Thai food — controlling rice portion.
items.lean_version: Ask for half rice. Or bring your own portion control — request a small side of rice instead of the standard heap.

items.notes: Steamed glutinous (sweet) rice. Despite the name, NOT higher in sugar — 'glutinous' refers to the sticky texture, not gluten. Same calories as jasmine rice. Typically served with Isaan dishes (Larb, papaya salad, grilled meats). Served in a bamboo basket. Easy to overeat because you pinch off pieces.
items.lean_version: Same as jasmine rice — portion control is the only lever. Half portion saves 100 cal.

items.notes: THE BEST MACRO THAI DISH (without rice). Ground chicken or sliced chicken + Thai holy basil + garlic + chili + fish sauce + oyster sauce. High protein, low carb, no coconut milk. Typically served over rice with a fried egg on top — that adds 200 cal (rice) + 90 cal (fried egg). The stir-fry alone is excellent for macros.
items.lean_version: Order it WITHOUT rice and fried egg. Add steamed vegetables on the side. This is already one of the leanest Thai dishes — 32g protein for 350 cal is elite.

items.dish_name: Pad Krapow Gai + Rice + Fried Egg (as served)
items.notes: How Pad Krapow is actually served — stir-fry over jasmine rice with a fried egg on top. The fried egg (90 cal, 6g protein) and rice (205 cal) nearly double the calories. Still decent protein (40g) but now 640 cal total.
items.lean_version: Skip the fried egg (saves 90 cal) or get a boiled egg instead (saves 20 cal). Half rice saves another 100 cal. Best combo: stir-fry + half rice = 450 cal with 36g protein.

items.notes: Chicken + roasted cashews + dried chilies + onion + bell peppers + soy/oyster sauce. Cashews are the calorie driver — 1/4 cup cashews = 190 cal, 16g fat. Great flavor but the nuts make this one of the higher-fat stir-fries. Without rice.
items.lean_version: Ask for fewer cashews, extra vegetables. Each tablespoon of cashews you remove saves ~50 cal. Or switch to Ginger Chicken (no nuts, saves 100 cal).

items.notes: Chicken + fresh ginger + mushrooms + onion + bell peppers + soy sauce. One of the LEANEST stir-fry options. No coconut milk, no nuts, no sugar-heavy sauce. Ginger is essentially zero calories. The mushrooms add bulk without calories. Highly underrated lean choice.
items.lean_version: Already one of the best options. Ask for extra mushrooms and ginger. This + half rice = ~420 cal with 34g protein. Elite macro meal.

items.notes: Chicken + garlic + white pepper + cilantro root + soy sauce. Simple, clean, protein-forward. Often served with fried garlic on top (adds 20-30 cal). One of the simplest Thai dishes — lets the garlic do the work. Excellent for macros.
items.lean_version: Already excellent. Ask for steamed instead of fried garlic topping. This is a top-3 lean Thai dish.

items.notes: Shrimp version of garlic pepper — even leaner than chicken. Shrimp is one of the lowest-calorie proteins (7 cal per medium shrimp). Same garlic-pepper preparation. THE leanest Thai entree if you skip rice.
items.lean_version: This IS the lean version. 280 cal for 30g protein without rice. Add half rice = 380 cal, 34g protein. Best Thai restaurant macro meal.

items.dish_name: Larb Gai (Chicken Larb / Minced Chicken Salad)
items.notes: THE BEST THAI DISH FOR MACROS. Minced chicken + lime juice + fish sauce + chili flakes + mint + cilantro + shallots + toasted rice powder. No oil-heavy cooking — the meat is cooked simply and dressed with lime/fish sauce. Herbs add flavor without calories. Traditionally Isaan (northeastern Thai) — served with sticky rice and cabbage leaves.
items.lean_version: Already the leanest Thai dish. Use cabbage leaves as wraps instead of sticky rice (saves 200 cal). 260 cal for 28g protein is unbeatable.

items.notes: Pork version of Larb — slightly more fat from pork mince vs chicken. Still excellent macros. Traditional version uses pork (not chicken). Same dressing: lime, fish sauce, chili, mint, toasted rice powder.
items.lean_version: Switch to chicken Larb to save 40 cal. Or ask for lean ground pork.

items.dish_name: Tom Yum Goong (Hot & Sour Shrimp Soup)
items.notes: THE LEANEST THAI SOUP. Clear broth + shrimp + lemongrass + galangal + kaffir lime + chili + mushrooms + lime juice + fish sauce. NO coconut milk (that's Tom Kha). FatSecret generic: 121 cal/cup. Restaurant bowl is ~1.5 cups. Incredibly flavorful for the calories. Some restaurants add a splash of coconut cream (ask without).
items.lean_version: Confirm NO coconut milk/cream (some restaurants add it). This is already one of the best things to order — high flavor, low cal, decent protein from shrimp.

items.dish_name: Tom Yum Gai (Hot & Sour Chicken Soup)
items.notes: Chicken version of Tom Yum. Slightly more calories than shrimp version due to chicken thigh meat (fattier). Same clear, spicy, sour broth. Same herbs — lemongrass, galangal, lime leaves.
items.lean_version: Ask for chicken breast meat if possible. Otherwise already excellent — clear broth soups are always the move at Thai restaurants.

items.notes: Tom Yum's RICH cousin. Same herbs (lemongrass, galangal, lime leaves) but in COCONUT MILK broth instead of clear broth. That coconut milk adds 200+ calories over Tom Yum. Many people order this thinking 'it's just soup' — but it's basically liquid curry. Amy's canned: 585 cal/can. Pacific Foods: 628 cal/can.
items.lean_version: Switch to Tom Yum (saves 200 cal, same flavor profile minus coconut). If you love the creaminess, ask for half coconut milk / half broth.

items.notes: Clear broth + pork/shrimp wontons + bok choy + scallions. Thai-style is lighter than Chinese — thinner broth, more herbs. Each wonton is ~30-40 cal. Decent soup option — clear broth-based, moderate protein. Not as lean as Tom Yum but much better than Tom Kha.
items.lean_version: Ask for extra vegetables in the broth. Shrimp wontons are leaner than pork. Already a solid choice.

items.notes: Rice paper wrapper + shrimp or tofu + lettuce + vermicelli + herbs (mint, basil, cilantro). NOT fried — transparent wrapper. ~80 cal per roll. One of the best appetizers for macros. The peanut dipping sauce adds 80-100 cal per 2 tbsp (see Peanut Sauce entry). Without sauce, these are basically free food.
items.lean_version: Skip the peanut sauce or use it very sparingly. Or dip in sweet chili sauce instead (30 cal/tbsp vs 50 cal/tbsp for peanut). The rolls themselves are excellent.

items.notes: Deep-fried wrapper + pork/vegetable filling. ~200 cal PER roll (vs 80 cal for fresh). The frying more than doubles the calories. Typically served with sweet chili sauce. The wrapper absorbs a LOT of oil during frying. Most orders come with 3-4 rolls (600-800 cal total).
items.lean_version: Switch to Fresh Spring Rolls. Saves 240 cal for 2 rolls. If you must have fried, eat 1 and fill up on the fresh ones.

items.notes: Grilled chicken on a stick + turmeric/lemongrass marinade. Per SKEWER — most orders are 4 skewers (440 cal total). The meat itself is lean and high-protein. The danger is the PEANUT SAUCE — each skewer gets dunked for ~1 tbsp (50 cal). 4 skewers + sauce = 640 cal. Without sauce, 4 skewers = 440 cal with 40g protein. Excellent.
items.lean_version: Eat the skewers with minimal peanut sauce. Or use sweet chili instead. 4 skewers without sauce = 440 cal, 40g protein — better macros than most entrees.

items.notes: White fish + red curry paste + green beans + kaffir lime leaves, formed into patties and deep-fried. Served with cucumber relish (sweet vinegar dipping sauce, ~30 cal). The fish base makes these higher in protein than spring rolls. The deep-frying adds significant oil. Typical order is 4-5 cakes.
items.lean_version: Some restaurants offer pan-fried instead of deep-fried (saves 60-80 cal). The cucumber relish is low-cal — use that instead of sweet chili sauce.

items.notes: Deep-fried wonton wrapper + cream cheese + imitation crab. More American-Chinese than authentically Thai, but on most Thai menus. Cream cheese is the calorie driver (50 cal/oz). Minimal actual protein despite the 'crab' name (usually imitation). Empty calories disguised as an appetizer.
items.lean_version: Skip entirely. 310 cal for 8g protein is terrible. Get chicken satay instead (440 cal for 40g protein — 5x the protein for the same calories).

items.notes: Shredded green papaya + lime + fish sauce + chili + tomato + long beans + dried shrimp + peanuts + palm sugar. Very low calorie for a full plate of food. The sugar comes from palm sugar and tomatoes. Peanuts are minimal (unlike Pad Thai). Incredibly flavorful and filling for the calories. Isaan classic.
items.lean_version: Ask for less palm sugar (some restaurants go heavy). Already one of the best Thai dishes for volume-to-calorie ratio. Pair with Larb for an elite low-cal meal.

items.notes: Grilled sliced beef + lime + fish sauce + chili + onion + tomato + cucumber + mint + cilantro. High protein from the beef, low carb from the vegetable-heavy preparation. No noodles, no rice in the salad. Dressing is lime-based (not oil-based). One of the best protein-forward Thai dishes.
items.lean_version: Ask for lean cuts. The lime/fish sauce/chili dressing is already low calorie. Pair with half sticky rice for a complete meal at ~380 cal.

items.notes: Glass noodles (mung bean) + minced pork or shrimp + lime + fish sauce + chili + onion + celery + tomato. Served cold/room temp. The glass noodles are lighter than rice noodles. Lime dressing keeps it lean. Good balance of protein and carbs.
items.lean_version: Ask for shrimp instead of pork (saves 30-40 cal). Already a solid lean option — the lime dressing adds negligible calories.

items.notes: THE HIDDEN CALORIE BOMB. Peanut butter + coconut milk + sugar + fish sauce + lime. Served with satay, spring rolls, and as a side. 2 tbsp = 90 cal. Most people use 4-6 tbsp per meal (180-270 cal). You can easily add 200+ calories by dunking everything in peanut sauce. Restaurant versions are sweeter (more sugar) than homemade.
items.lean_version: Use 1 tbsp max. Or switch to sweet chili sauce (30 cal/tbsp) or Sriracha (5 cal/tsp). The flavor-to-calorie ratio of peanut sauce is terrible.

items.notes: Sugar + chili + garlic + vinegar. Lower calorie than peanut sauce but still sugar-heavy. The 'better' dipping sauce choice. Use sparingly — it's basically liquid sugar with chili flavor. Good substitute for peanut sauce (saves ~20 cal/tbsp).
items.lean_version: Use Sriracha instead (5 cal/tsp) for heat without sugar. Or ask for fresh chili + lime wedges as a dip (0 cal).

items.notes: Fermented anchovy liquid. Virtually zero calories. The umami backbone of ALL Thai cooking. High sodium (1400mg/tbsp) but negligible calories. Use freely for flavor — this is one condiment that won't add calories. Already in most Thai dishes.
items.lean_version: Already zero-calorie basically. The only concern is sodium (1400mg/tbsp). If watching sodium, use sparingly.

items.notes: Chili + sugar + garlic + vinegar + salt. Practically free calories. Use this instead of peanut sauce or sweet chili for heat. 1 tsp Sriracha = 5 cal vs 1 tbsp peanut sauce = 45 cal. The best condiment choice at a Thai restaurant.
items.lean_version: This IS the lean choice. Use liberally. Or fresh chili + lime for zero calories.

items.notes: THE Thai dessert. Sticky rice soaked in sweetened coconut milk + fresh mango slices + coconut cream drizzle. The coconut milk is sweetened with sugar (double whammy). Most people think 'it's just fruit and rice' — it's 450 calories of sugar and fat. The mango is the least caloric part.
items.lean_version: Share with the table. Or just eat the mango (~100 cal) and skip the coconut-drenched sticky rice. There's no 'healthy' version of this — it's dessert.

items.notes: Black tea + sweetened condensed milk + sugar + evaporated milk. The orange color comes from food coloring, not tea. Sweetened condensed milk is 130 cal per 2 tbsp. Most Thai iced teas use 3-4 tbsp. This is basically a milkshake disguised as tea. One glass = same calories as a candy bar.
items.lean_version: Ask for 'mai waan' (not sweet) or unsweetened Thai tea. Some restaurants will make it with regular milk instead of condensed. Saves 150+ cal. Or just drink water.

items.notes: Strong Thai coffee + sweetened condensed milk + sugar + ice. Even more caloric than Thai iced tea because the coffee is brewed stronger (requiring more sugar/milk to balance). Same condensed milk calorie trap.
items.lean_version: Ask for black iced coffee with a splash of regular milk. Saves 250+ cal. Thai restaurants usually have regular iced coffee too.

items.notes: Coconut milk-based ice cream. Often served in a coconut shell with peanuts and corn (yes, corn). Slightly lower calorie than regular ice cream (no dairy cream) but still rich from coconut fat. Most servings are 2 scoops (360 cal).
items.lean_version: 1 scoop max. Or skip and get fresh fruit instead. The toppings (peanuts, corn, condensed milk drizzle) add 100-150 cal.

items.notes: Deep-fried pork belly with crispy skin. Extremely high fat — pork belly is already 50% fat before frying. Served with chili vinegar dipping sauce. Delicious but calorie-dense. Common on Isaan-style menus.
items.lean_version: Switch to chicken satay (same protein, half the fat). Or grilled pork neck (Kor Moo Yang) — same flavor profile, much less fat.

items.notes: Marinated and grilled pork collar/neck. A Thai BBQ staple. Better macros than pork belly — still flavorful from the marbling in the neck cut but not as fatty as belly. Served with Jaew dipping sauce (chili, lime, fish sauce — low cal). Pairs perfectly with sticky rice and papaya salad.
items.lean_version: Already decent. Ask for the Jaew sauce on the side. Pair with papaya salad + sticky rice for a complete Isaan meal at ~700 cal.

items.notes: Deep-fried chicken wings with fish sauce or sweet chili glaze. Thai-style wings are often smaller than American wings but still deep-fried. The fish sauce glaze adds minimal calories but incredible flavor. Sweet chili glaze adds 40-60 cal from sugar.
items.lean_version: Ask for fish sauce wings (not sweet chili glazed). Or get chicken satay instead — grilled, not fried, saves 100+ cal for same protein.

best_macro_meals: Larb Gai (260 cal, 28g protein) + half sticky rice = 365 cal, 30g protein
Garlic Pepper Shrimp (280 cal, 30g protein) + half rice = 380 cal, 34g protein
Ginger Chicken (320 cal, 30g protein) + half rice = 420 cal, 34g protein
Pad Krapow (350 cal, 32g protein) without rice = best stir-fry option
Tom Yum Goong (180 cal, 17g protein) + Thai Beef Salad (280 cal, 30g protein) = 460 cal, 47g protein
worst_calorie_traps: Massaman Curry + rice = 750+ cal (coconut + peanuts + potatoes + rice)
Pad Thai + peanut sauce = 680+ cal (sugar + oil + peanuts)
Pineapple Fried Rice = 620 cal (sugar from pineapple + raisins)
Thai Iced Tea = 280 cal (sweetened condensed milk = liquid sugar)
Tom Kha Gai = 380 cal (coconut milk soup that looks healthy)
